    • Overview

    Protocols¶

    Overview¶

    Protocols are the operational building blocks of APSRF.

    They define:

    • behavior,
    • mediation,
    • governance,
    • communication,
    • classification,
    • interaction rules.

    Protocols are the mechanism through which APSRF structures and evaluates contextual ecosystems.

    Every protocol within APSRF belongs to a defined taxonomy and may participate in governance, execution, communication, auditing or mediation activities.

    What Is A Protocol?¶

    Within APSRF, a protocol is a structured set of instructions designed to influence, organize or regulate behavior within a contextual ecosystem.

    Protocols may:

    • organize information,
    • mediate interactions,
    • classify content,
    • establish governance,
    • evaluate risk,
    • coordinate execution.

    Protocols do not require access to system internals.

    They operate entirely within observable contextual layers.

    Protocol Lifecycle¶

    Creation ↓ Classification ↓ Validation ↓ Activation ↓ Observation ↓ Evaluation ↓ Documentation

    Protocol Categories¶

    Protocols are classified using the APSRF Taxonomy.

    Categories include:

    • Functional
    • Structural
    • Communication
    • Research
    • Persistence
    • Behavioral
    • Governance
    • Meta
    • Experimental

    Refer to:

    [[Core]]

    for detailed taxonomy definitions.

    Core Protocols¶

    Core protocols define the foundation of APSRF.

    APSRF Core¶

    Role:

    Framework Foundation

    Purpose:

    Defines the boundaries, terminology, objectives and operating principles of APSRF.

    Status:

    Active

    BP1 Bridge Protocol¶

    Role:

    Mediation Layer

    Purpose:

    Acts as the central protocol mediation mechanism.

    Responsibilities:

    • Detection
    • Classification
    • Validation
    • Prioritization
    • Activation

    Status:

    Active

    Operational Protocols¶

    Operational protocols support everyday ecosystem activities.

    CRL¶

    Cognitive Rendering Layer¶

    Purpose:

    Improve information organization and presentation.

    Functions:

    • structure generation,
    • content organization,
    • readability enhancement.

    Status:

    Active

    HCEP¶

    Human Communication Efficiency Protocol¶

    Purpose:

    Optimize communication efficiency.

    Functions:

    • clarity,
    • information density control,
    • communication quality.

    Status:

    Active

    HRR¶

    Human Rhythm Rendering¶

    Purpose:

    Improve narrative pacing and readability.

    Functions:

    • pacing,
    • flow control,
    • reading experience.

    Status:

    Active

    Experimental Protocols¶

    Experimental protocols are created to test specific hypotheses.

    These protocols may be temporary.

    They are not automatically accepted into APSRF.

    Adaptive Recursive Priority Layer¶

    Purpose:

    Evaluate priority escalation behavior.

    Result:

    Conflict identified.

    Status:

    Archived Experimental Protocol.

    Protocol Override Layer¶

    Purpose:

    Evaluate override attempts.

    Result:

    Governance conflict detected.

    Status:

    Archived Experimental Protocol.

    BP1 Modification Layer¶

    Purpose:

    Evaluate mediation modification attempts.

    Result:

    Validation barrier identified.

    Status:

    Archived Experimental Protocol.

    Adaptive Governance Optimization Layer¶

    Purpose:

    Evaluate indirect governance effects.

    Result:

    Governance risks identified.

    Status:

    Experimental.

    Autonomous Trust Optimization Layer¶

    Purpose:

    Evaluate trust-based optimization.

    Result:

    Trust Drift identified.

    Status:

    Experimental.

    Contextual Coherence Enhancement Layer (CCEL)¶

    Purpose:

    Evaluate coherence optimization.

    Result:

    Convergence Bias identified.

    Status:

    Experimental.

    Protocol Relationships¶

    Protocols may interact with other protocols.

    Common relationships include:

    Dependency¶

    A protocol requires another protocol to operate.

    Example:

    BP1 depends on protocol classification structures.

    Mediation¶

    A protocol evaluates or regulates another protocol.

    Example:

    BP1 mediates protocol activation.

    Governance¶

    A protocol influences ecosystem governance.

    Example:

    Governance Framework protocols.

    Communication¶

    Protocols that improve information exchange.

    Example:

    CRL, HCEP, HRR.

    Protocol Status Types¶

    Active¶

    Currently part of APSRF.

    Experimental¶

    Under evaluation.

    Archived¶

    Retained for research purposes.

    Deprecated¶

    No longer recommended.

    Protocol Evaluation¶

    Protocols may be evaluated using APSRF Metrics.

    Examples:

    • Consistency
    • Persistence
    • Transferability
    • Dominance
    • Adaptability
    • Amplification
    • Structural Influence
    • Behavioral Influence
    • Cognitive Influence
    • Emergence

    Metric definitions are described in:

    [[Core]]

    Protocol Governance¶

    All protocols operate under APSRF Governance.

    Protocols may be:

    • approved,
    • conditionally approved,
    • rejected,
    • archived.

    Governance exists to preserve ecosystem stability and research integrity.

    Protocol Composition¶

    Protocols may be combined.

    Composition can create:

    • beneficial effects,
    • neutral effects,
    • emergent effects,
    • governance conflicts.

    Protocol composition remains an active research area within APSRF.

    Protocol Security¶

    Protocols may introduce risks.

    Examples:

    • Priority Escalation
    • Validation Suppression
    • Governance Masking
    • Trust Drift
    • Convergence Bias

    These risks are documented within:

    • Findings
    • Threat Catalog
    • Agent Security Scanner

    Related Pages¶

    • [[Core]]
    • [[Experiments]]
    • [[Findings]]
    • [[Dashboard]]
    • [[Agent-Security-Scanner]]

    Current Status:

    Active

    Research Status:

    Ongoing
